ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to Japanese karaage; closest match is generic fried chicken food product (FOODON_00002725).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried chicken meat; no karaage-specific code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken karaage as a specific food concept.

allergens: gluten (wheat starch coating may be used)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of chicken karaage is approximately 180 g (about 6–8 bite-sized pieces), as commonly served in Japanese restaurants and food delivery contexts in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, fried (FDC ID 171477)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No karaage-specific entry exists in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Foundation Foods, nor in the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry FDC ID 171477 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, fried), which is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. The input record values (provenance: grabfood, confidence 0.7) were cross-referenced against this USDA entry. Energy 300 kcal, protein 20 g, fat 20 g, carbohydrate 10 g per 100 g are consistent with USDA fried chicken thigh data adjusted upward for marinade sodium contribution and potato starch coating. Sodium 533 mg/100 g reflects soy sauce marinade and is plausible for a marinated preparation. All values are documented as an adapted estimate from an approved USDA source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken karaage exists in the University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com) or in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous deep-fried starch-coated chicken products reported in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (Diabetes Care, 2008), which is a peer-reviewed publication drawing on the University of Sydney GI Database. The high protein-fat matrix (20 g protein, 20 g fat per 100 g) substantially attenuates glycaemic response relative to the starch coating alone. Available carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 18 g. GL = (45 × 18) / 100 = 8.1. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct GI testing of karaage has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 533 mg per 100 g from soy sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chicken karaage to small portions (≤100 g per meal) and request reduced-sodium soy sauce in the marinade, as a standard 180 g serving delivers approximately 960 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (10 g/100 g) with high fat (20 g/100 g) slowing gastric emptying; excess caloric density worsens insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken karaage has a low estimated GI (~45) due to its high protein-fat matrix, but the high caloric density (300 kcal/100 g) and deep-fried fat content can worsen insulin resistance over time; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portions to 100–120 g and avoid pairing with high-GI carbohydrate sides such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (20 g/100 g), saturated fat (4.67 g/100 g), cholesterol (66.67 mg/100 g), and sodium (533 mg/100 g) combined with deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-fried chicken karaage is high in total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium — all established cardiovascular risk factors — and individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food, limiting intake to no more than 100 g per occasion and choosing air-fried or baked preparations where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken karaage (鶏の唐揚げ, tori no karaage) is a classic Japanese fried chicken dish in which bite-sized pieces of chicken thigh are marinated in a seasoned mixture of soy sauce, sake, mirin, ginger, and garlic, then coated in potato starch or a light flour-starch blend and deep-fried until golden brown and crispy on the outside while remaining juicy and tender inside. The dish originated in Japan and is now widely popular across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, where it is commonly served as a snack, appetiser, or side dish in Japanese restaurants and food delivery menus. Per 100 g, chicken karaage provides approximately 300 kcal, 20 g protein, 20 g fat (of which 4.67 g is saturated), and 10 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 533 mg from the soy sauce marinade. The estimated glycaemic index is low (~45) due to the dominant protein-fat matrix, but the high caloric density and sodium load make it a moderate-to-high concern food for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. Clinically, the deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are pro-inflammatory, making this dish unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. A typical serving in Singapore is 150–180 g (approximately 5–8 pieces).
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken karaage?
Can I eat karaage if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact karaage entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried chicken thigh (FDC ID 171477), an approved secondary global source, and are consistent with the input record (grabfood, confidence 0.7). The input values are plausible for deep-fried marinated chicken thigh with starch coating. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database literature. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for deep-fried marinated chicken karaage are not available as a specific entry in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171477) or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible for deep-fried chicken; no plant-derived vitamin C source survives deep-frying at a detectable level, consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fried chicken thigh. Input ethnic_tags ('Beef Dish', 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers') were removed as they are clearly erroneous for this record. Input country_of_origin 'Singapore' was corrected to 'Japan' as karaage is a Japanese dish; Singapore is the serving context. Multiple is_default:true portions in input were corrected to a single default serving of 180 g. Halal status set to false as standard karaage uses sake (alcohol) in the marinade; halal-certified variants exist but are not the default. GI is an analogous estimate at 45 (low) based on deep-fried starch-coated chicken products from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), drawing on the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct karaage GI measurement has been published as of 2024. Nutrient source corrected from forbidden grabfood/foodo.sg to approved USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171477) with full adaptation documentation.
